nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, riverbanks, and seasonally flooded wetlands along the Amazon basin and other tropical lowland river systems

summary: The Amazon Sword is a large rosette plant from South America, recognizable by its broad, lance-shaped green leaves. It is widely used as a background specimen or solitary centerpiece in medium to large freshwater planted tanks.
careNotes: The Amazon Sword (Echinodorus grisebachii) is one of the most recognizable aquarium plants in the freshwater hobby, valued for its bold rosette of broad, lance-shaped leaves and its tolerance for a wide range of water conditions. Several older scientific names — including Echinodorus bleheri, E. bleherae, E. amazonicus, and E. paniculatus — are now generally treated as synonyms of E. grisebachii following DNA work published in 2008, though the older names still appear on some plant labels and in hobbyist discussions. Light requirements are often described as low to medium; the plant tends to grow more slowly and with looser leaf structure under dim conditions, and typically responds better with moderate illumination. It tolerates a temperature range of roughly 20–30 °C and is compatible with pH 6.5–7.5 and general hardness of 4–18 dGH, making it suitable for most community freshwater setups. It is a heavy root feeder and benefits from a nutrient-rich substrate along with root fertilizer tabs placed near the root zone, refreshed roughly once a month. At full size it commonly reaches 25–50 cm in height, so it suits background placement in tanks of 100 liters or larger; in smaller aquariums it tends to crowd out the layout over time. Newly purchased specimens often arrive with broader, rounder emersed-grown leaves (leaves grown above the waterline before harvest) that typically melt back within a few weeks as the plant shifts to producing its submerged form — longer, more lance-shaped leaves. This transitional melt is normal and not a sign of poor health.
breedingNotes: The Amazon Sword propagates most readily through adventitious plantlets — small daughter plants that form spontaneously along the long flower stalks the plant sends up. Once each plantlet has developed a small rosette and visible roots, it can be snipped from the stalk and planted directly in the substrate. Mature plants also spread through side runners and can be divided at the crown where multiple rosettes develop. Flowering and seed production are possible under high light and emersed or humid conditions, but these rarely occur in a fully submerged aquarium setup.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica page for Echinodorus grisebachii 'Bleherae' (the standard Amazon Sword form). Origin South America, height 20–50+ cm, light demand low, CO2 demand low, recommended as solitary plant for larger aquariums. Manifest-supplied search URL (https://tropica.com/en/search?q=Echinodorus%20grisebachii) returned only the site footer; navigated to the canonical plant-details page via Tropica's own search.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirmed temperature 20–28 °C (68–82 °F), moderate light preferred (tolerates low), CO2 optional, heavy root feeder, runners/side-shoot propagation, background/centerpiece placement, easy care level.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (50+ cm / 20+ in), background placement, heavy root feeder needing 2–3 root tabs at planting plus monthly replenishment, easy care level.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Echinodorus grisebachii
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Alismataceae; 2008 DNA work consolidating E. amazonicus and E. bleherae under E. grisebachii), substrate depth recommendation (~10 cm), and propagation modes (crown division, plantlets on stalks, occasional flowering).

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Echinodorus bleheri
additional.notes: Used to fill temperature range (20–30 °C), pH (6.5–7.5), and hardness (4–18 dGH) where Tropica/Modern Aquarium are silent. Propagation: adventitious plantlets on flower stalks plus daughter plants around the rosette.
